title: TLS Certificate Expiry
description: Async check: opens a TLS connection to :443 and reports the certificate validity window, expiry, self-signing, and incomplete chains.
evidence: Executed in async-checks.ts via Node tls.connect().
riskImpact: An expired or self-signed certificate breaks HTTPS trust and exposes users to MITM attacks.
explanation: TLS certificates are short-lived by design. The scan opens a TLS connection (not just an HTTP request) so it can inspect the leaf certificate and chain directly.
fixSteps: Renew the certificate via your CA (Let's Encrypt is free).
Enable auto-renewal (certbot, Caddy, or hosting-provider automation).
Verify the chain: openssl s_client -connect example.com:443 -showcerts.

title: Weak TLS Protocol Version
description: Async check: reports whether the negotiated TLS protocol is TLS 1.2+ or older (TLS 1.0/1.1/SSLv3).
evidence: Executed via Node tls.connect() with negotiated protocol logged.
riskImpact: Older TLS versions have known attacks (POODLE, BEAST, ROBOT) and lack modern cipher suites.
explanation: TLS 1.0 and 1.1 have been deprecated by IETF (RFC 8996). Most browsers and APIs no longer support them.
fixSteps: Disable TLS 1.0 and 1.1 on the server.
Keep TLS 1.2 and 1.3 enabled.
Prefer AEAD ciphers (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305).

title: RSA Key Size < 2048 bits
description: Modern TLS certs should use RSA ≥ 2048 bits (≥ 3072 recommended).
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cert-key-size-rsa.
riskImpact: Reissue the certificate with RSA 2048 or 3072
explanation: Modern TLS certs should use RSA ≥ 2048 bits (≥ 3072 recommended).
fixSteps: Reissue the certificate with RSA 2048 or 3072

title: ECDSA Key Size < 256 bits
description: ECDSA keys should use NIST P-256 or larger.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cert-key-size-ecdsa.
riskImpact: Reissue with P-256 or P-384
explanation: ECDSA keys should use NIST P-256 or larger.
fixSteps: Reissue with P-256 or P-384

title: Certificate Signed with SHA-1
description: SHA-1 signatures are deprecated by all major browsers and CAs.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cert-sha1-sig.
riskImpact: Reissue with SHA-256
explanation: SHA-1 signatures are deprecated by all major browsers and CAs.
fixSteps: Reissue with SHA-256

title: Self-Signed Certificate in Production
description: Self-signed certs are fine for dev but break trust in production.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cert-self-signed.
riskImpact: Use a publicly trusted CA (Let's Encrypt, DigiCert, etc.)
explanation: Self-signed certs are fine for dev but break trust in production.
fixSteps: Use a publicly trusted CA (Let's Encrypt, DigiCert, etc.)

title: OCSP Stapling Not Enabled
description: OCSP stapling avoids leaking browsing activity to the CA's responder.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-ocsp-stapling-missing.
riskImpact: Enable OCSP stapling in your web server config
explanation: OCSP stapling avoids leaking browsing activity to the CA's responder.
fixSteps: Enable OCSP stapling in your web server config

title: Certificate Transparency Log Missing
description: CAs submit public certs to CT logs. Submitting detects mis-issuance.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-ct-log-missing.
riskImpact: Submit your cert to CT logs (most CAs do this automatically)
explanation: CAs submit public certs to CT logs. Submitting detects mis-issuance.
fixSteps: Submit your cert to CT logs (most CAs do this automatically)

title: HPKP Header Present
description: Public Key Pinning is deprecated and dangerous if misconfigured.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-hpkp-deprecated.
riskImpact: Remove HPKP; rely on CA / CT log instead
explanation: Public Key Pinning is deprecated and dangerous if misconfigured.
fixSteps: Remove HPKP; rely on CA / CT log instead

title: TLS 1.3 Not Supported
description: TLS 1.3 reduces handshake latency and improves forward secrecy. Negotiation fails back to 1.2 if 1.3 isn't enabled.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-tls-1-3-not-supported.
riskImpact: Enable TLS 1.3 in your server config
explanation: TLS 1.3 reduces handshake latency and improves forward secrecy. Negotiation fails back to 1.2 if 1.3 isn't enabled.
fixSteps: Enable TLS 1.3 in your server config

title: HSTS Preload List Status
description: Even with Strict-Transport-Security set, browsers are not bound by it on first visit. Submitting to the HSTS preload list guarantees TLS-only from the very first request.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-hsts-preload-status.
riskImpact: Without preload, the first request to your domain can be downgraded by an attacker who strips the HSTS header.
explanation: Even with Strict-Transport-Security set, browsers are not bound by it on first visit. Submitting to the HSTS preload list guarantees TLS-only from the very first request.
fixSteps: Set Strict-Transport-Security with includeSubDomains and a long max-age (>= 31536000).
Submit to https://hstspreload.org once stable.

title: OCSP Must-Staple Extension Missing
description: The TLS feature must-staple (RFC 7633) lets a certificate signal that stapled OCSP responses are mandatory, protecting against OCSP-stapling-stripping attacks.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cert-must-staple-missing.
riskImpact: Without must-staple, a network attacker can strip any stapled OCSP response and force clients to fall back to soft-fail behavior.
explanation: The TLS feature must-staple (RFC 7633) lets a certificate signal that stapled OCSP responses are mandatory, protecting against OCSP-stapling-stripping attacks.
fixSteps: Reissue the certificate with the id-pe-tlsfeature extension (1.3.6.1.5.5.7.1.24) set to status_request.
Enable OCSP stapling in the server (ssl_stapling on for Nginx).

title: Subject Alternative Name (SAN) Missing
description: Modern browsers and APIs only trust certificates that include Subject Alternative Name (SAN). The legacy CN field is ignored.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cert-san-missing.
riskImpact: A certificate without SAN is treated as untrusted by current browsers, breaking HTTPS for end users.
explanation: Modern browsers and APIs only trust certificates that include Subject Alternative Name (SAN). The legacy CN field is ignored.
fixSteps: Reissue the certificate with all required hostnames listed in the SAN extension.
Use acme.sh or certbot with -d flags to ensure SAN is populated.

title: Key Usage Extension Wrong or Absent
description: X.509 certificates must carry the Key Usage extension declaring digitalSignature and keyEncipherment for TLS server certs. Wrong or absent KU is rejected by strict verifiers.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cert-key-usage-wrong.
riskImpact: Browsers and libraries that enforce KU may refuse to negotiate, breaking HTTPS for users.
explanation: X.509 certificates must carry the Key Usage extension declaring digitalSignature and keyEncipherment for TLS server certs. Wrong or absent KU is rejected by strict verifiers.
fixSteps: Regenerate the CSR so the CA includes digitalSignature and keyEncipherment.
Inspect with: openssl x509 -in cert.pem -noout -ext keyUsage,extendedKeyUsage.

title: Expired CA in Certificate Chain
description: If any intermediate or root CA in the chain is expired, browsers cannot validate the chain and HTTPS fails.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cert-expired-ca-chain.
riskImpact: End users see NET::ERR_CERT_DATE_INVALID; APIs cannot complete TLS handshakes.
explanation: If any intermediate or root CA in the chain is expired, browsers cannot validate the chain and HTTPS fails.
fixSteps: Reissue the leaf with the current cross-signed intermediate from your CA.
Test the full chain: openssl s_client -connect <host>:443 -showcerts.

title: 3DES Cipher Suite Offered
description: Triple DES (3DES / DES-CBC3) is slow and broken by SWEET32. Modern clients should never negotiate it.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cipher-3des-offered.
riskImpact: Attackers can recover plaintext from long-lived TLS sessions using the SWEET32 birthday-bound attack on 3DES.
explanation: Triple DES (3DES / DES-CBC3) is slow and broken by SWEET32. Modern clients should never negotiate it.
fixSteps: Remove all ECDHE-RSA-DES-CBC3-SHA / RSA-DES-CBC3-SHA cipher strings.
Use only AEAD ciphers (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305).

title: RC4 Cipher Suite Offered
description: RC4 is cryptographically broken and prohibited by RFC 7465. Any server still offering RC4 should be reconfigured immediately.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cipher-rc4-offered.
riskImpact: Long-term plaintext recovery attacks on RC4 are practical; credentials and session cookies can be exfiltrated.
explanation: RC4 is cryptographically broken and prohibited by RFC 7465. Any server still offering RC4 should be reconfigured immediately.
fixSteps: Delete any ECDHE-RSA-RC4-SHA / RC4-SHA cipher string from your config.
Force-prefer AES-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305.

title: NULL Cipher Suite Offered (No Encryption)
description: NULL cipher suites (e.g., TLS_RSA_WITH_NULL_SHA) provide authentication but zero encryption. They are catastrophic in production.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cipher-null-offered.
riskImpact: All traffic is sent in cleartext, exposing credentials, session tokens, and user data to any on-path adversary.
explanation: NULL cipher suites (e.g., TLS_RSA_WITH_NULL_SHA) provide authentication but zero encryption. They are catastrophic in production.
fixSteps: Disable any NULL cipher in your server config (e.g., ssl_ciphers HIGH:!aNULL:!eNULL:!NULL).
Verify with: nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443 <host>.

title: EXPORT-Grade Cipher Suite Offered
description: EXPORT cipher suites (40/56-bit keys) are intentionally weak and exploitable by FREAK/Logjam. Any server still offering them is misconfigured.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cipher-export-offered.
riskImpact: Attackers can downgrade sessions to a 512-bit key and recover session secrets within hours.
explanation: EXPORT cipher suites (40/56-bit keys) are intentionally weak and exploitable by FREAK/Logjam. Any server still offering them is misconfigured.
fixSteps: Strip EXPORT from your cipher list (ssl_ciphers ... !EXPORT).
Ensure DHE parameters are at least 2048 bits to also close Logjam.

title: Anonymous DH Key Exchange Offered
description: Anonymous cipher suites (ADH / AECDH) provide no server authentication, allowing trivial MITM attacks.
evidence: Async / body / header check for tls-cipher-anonymous-dh.
riskImpact: An attacker can intercept the session without detection, defeating the entire purpose of TLS.
explanation: Anonymous cipher suites (ADH / AECDH) provide no server authentication, allowing trivial MITM attacks.
fixSteps: Disable anonymous KEX: ssl_ciphers ... !aNULL:!eNULL.
Require server certificates and ECDHE/ DHE-RSA key exchange.
